questions.question: A `protected` field is accessible from where?
options: Within the same class and package only
Within the same class, package, and subclasses (even in other packages)
questions.explanation: Protected access allows: same class, same package, and subclasses in any package. It does NOT allow access from unrelated classes in other packages.

questions.question: What is the access level when no modifier is specified (default access)?
options: Package-private — accessible within the same package only
questions.explanation: Default (package-private) access means visible to all classes in the same package but invisible outside it. There is no keyword for this — you simply omit the modifier.

questions.question: Which is NOT a requirement for making a class immutable?
options: All fields are private and final
Defensive copies for mutable field types
questions.explanation: Immutability requires private final fields, no setters, and defensive copies. Serializable is unrelated — it's for object serialization, not immutability.

questions.question: In JavaBeans convention, what is the getter name for a `boolean active` field?
questions.explanation: JavaBeans convention uses isX() for primitive boolean fields and getX() for Boolean wrapper type and all other types.

questions.question: Why is encapsulation beneficial even within a single-developer project?
options: It makes the code run faster
It forces you to write more classes
It lets you change internal implementation without breaking dependent code
It is required by the Java compiler
questions.explanation: Encapsulation decouples the public API from internal details. You can refactor field types, add validation, or change storage without modifying callers.
